coredata.dc:title: Facile one-pot synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots by mild hydrothermal method and their applications in detection of Cr(VI) ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Shaohua
Cui, Jiale
Huang, Jingbin
Tian, Boshi
Jia, Fan
Wang, Zhenling
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with well-distribution size and strong blue emission were successfully synthesized via a simple mild hydrothermal strategy using citric acid and ethylenediamine as co-precursors. The highly fluorescent N-CDs exhibit high fluorescence quantum yield (QY, 58.6%), excitation-independent emission behavior, and good photostability. The experimental results showed that the N-CDs can be served as a fluorescent sensing platform for detection of Cr(VI) ions due to the effective fluorescence quenching effect of Cr(VI) ions. The quenching mechanism probably arises from the inner filter effect (IFE) and the electron transfer due to the strong interactions between functional groups (COOH, OH and NH2 groups) of the N-CDs and Cr(VI) ions. It is also found that the N-CDs showed high sensitivity toward Cr(VI) ions with a detection limit of 0.26 μM. Moreover, the obtained N-CD can be employed as chemsensor to detect Cr (VI) in real river water samples, which have potential applications in the environmental water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped
Carbon dots
Fluorescent
Detection
Chromium (VI) ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) in Environment Water

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21601213 , 51572303 ), Innovation Scientists and Technicians Troop Construction Projects of Henan Province ( 2013259 ), the Key Program of Committee of Education of Henan Province ( 17A150055 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Low-resolution and (B) high-resolution TEM image of N-CDs, the inset of (A) shows the particle size distribution histogram.
(A) XRD pattern and (B) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs.
(A) XPS spectrum of N-CDs. (B-D) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, O1s and N1 s, respectively.

(A) UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs. The inset shows the photograph of N-CDs in aqueous solution under UV lamp (left, λex = 365 nm) and daylight (right). (B) Emission spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelength.

(A) Fluorescence emission of N-CDs in the presence of different concentration of Cr(VI) (from top to bottom: 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 14, 18, 24, 30, 40, 50, 70 and 90 μM), the inset of (A) displays the photograph of N-CDs solution before (left) and after (right) addition of Cr(VI) ions (60 μM) under UV lamp (λ ex = 365 nm). (B) The plot of the fluorescence intensity ration of N-CDs versus the concentration of Cr(VI), where F0 and F refers to the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs in absence and presence of Cr(VI), respectively. The inset of (B) shows the liner relationship between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Cr(VI).

(A) Normalized fluorescence intensity (λ ex = 350 nm) of aqueous N-CDs solution (3.0 μg/mL) in the presence of 30 μM of various metal ions. (B) Selective PL response of aqueous N-CDs solution toward 30 μM Cr(VI) (black bars), and interference of 30 μM other metal ions with 30 μM Cr(VI) (red bars). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) and excitation spectrum of N-CDs. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, Cr(VI) and the mixture solution of N-CDs and Cr(VI) ions.

Fluorescence emission of N-CDs in river upon addition of various concentration of Cr(VI) (from top to bottom: 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32, 36 and 40 μM). (B) The relationship between F0–F/F0 and the concentration of Cr(VI) of river water.
Schematic illustration of fluorescent N-CDs for detection of Cr(VI) ions.
Comparison of N-doped carbon dots by different starting materials and synthetic method in detection of metal ions and other objects.

head.ce:title: Facile one-pot synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots by mild hydrothermal method and their applications in detection of Cr(VI) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou, 466001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials, The Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials and Applications, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou, 466001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials
The Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Functional Materials and Applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with well-distribution size and strong blue emission were successfully synthesized via a simple mild hydrothermal strategy using citric acid and ethylenediamine as co-precursors. The highly fluorescent N-CDs exhibit high fluorescence quantum yield (QY, 58.6%), excitation-independent emission behavior, and good photostability. The experimental results showed that the N-CDs can be served as a fluorescent sensing platform for detection of Cr(VI) ions due to the effective fluorescence quenching effect of Cr(VI) ions. The quenching mechanism probably arises from the inner filter effect (IFE) and the electron transfer due to the strong interactions between functional groups (COOH, OH and NH2 groups) of the N-CDs and Cr(VI) ions. It is also found that the N-CDs showed high sensitivity toward Cr(VI) ions with a detection limit of 0.26 μM. Moreover, the obtained N-CD can be employed as chemsensor to detect Cr (VI) in real river water samples, which have potential applications in the environmental water.

Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield (58.6%), which prepared by a simple, one-pot mild hydrothermal method, could be served as a fluorescent sensing platform for fast and effective detection of Cr(VI) ions.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium (VI) [Cr(VI)] is recognized as a more toxic heavy metal ion due to its high carcinogenicity [1–3]. Thus, the concentration of Cr(VI) in drink water is strictly limited to an allowable concentration range (below 0.96 μM [4]) by many nations. Therefore, sensitive detection of Cr(VI) ions is important for environmental. Several methods have been successfully developed to detect Cr(VI) ions, such as atomic absorption spectrometry [5], chromatography [6], X-ray fluorescence spectrometry [7], anodic stripping voltammetry [8], etc. Although these traditional methods can effectively detect Cr(VI) ions, they require expensive equipment and complicate sample pretreatments, which limit their applications in real Cr(VI) detection. Thus, it is of great importance to develop a simple, fast, method for detection of Cr(VI) ions.

Carbon dots (CDs) have drawn much attention since they were discovered in 2004 due to their unique properties [9–11]. Compared with the traditional toxic metal-based quantum dots (such as CdSe, CdTe, and CdS, etc.), CDs exhibit several attractive features including simple and cheap synthetic routes, excellent biocompatibility and water solubility, etc [12,13]. Therefore, CDs have been widely applied in bioimaging, photocatalysis, sensors and so forth [14–17]. Especially, the applications of CDs in metal ions detection have drawn more attentions in recent years, because the fluorescent CDs usually have high quantum yield (QY) and can be quenched by special metal ions [18–26]. In order to improve the QY of CDs, nitrogen doping is considered to be the most effective strategy at present because the small atomic size and rich valence electrons of nitrogen atom can bond with the carbon atoms [10,27]. Moreover, nitrogen-doped CDs can effectively adjust their electronic characteristics, surface and local chemical reactivity [28,29]. Recently, many efforts have been made to prepare nitrogen-doped CDs for detecting metal ions [1,30–34]. However, the current synthetic methods usually involve time consuming procedures (>4 h) and high temperature (>180 °C). In addition, up to date, the detection of Cr(VI) based on N-CDs chemsensor have been rarely reported. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a facile, sensitive method to detect Cr(VI) ions based on N-CDs.

On the other hand, the fluorescence quenching mechanism induced by metal ions is vague at present. Most researchers attribute the quenching mechanism to the electron, charge or energy transfer due to the interaction between CDs and metal ions [18]. The inner filter effect (IFE) is considered another reason for the fluorescence quenching [35]. IFE, which arises from the absorption of the excitation or emission light by absorbers in the detection system [1], has been proven to enhance the sensitivity compared to other conventional fluorescent quenching mechanisms [18,36–38]. Moreover, the detection system based on IFE is simple and flexible because it does not need covalent interaction between receptor and fluorophore.

In this study, we reported a facile, and mild hydrothermal method (150 °C, 2 h) for synthesis of highly luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for detection of Cr(VI) ions. The obtained N-CDs exhibit a high quantum yield (58.6%) and strong emission around 465 nm under the excitation of 350 nm UV light. The fluorescence emission of N-CDs can be effectively quenched upon addition of Cr(VI) ions. The experimental results exhibit that the cause of the fluorescence quenching can be attributed to the IFE effect and the electron transfer due to the interaction between N-CDs and Cr(VI) ions. These findings indicate that the N-CDs can be used as an effective sensor for detecting of Cr(VI) ions in aqueous solution.

Anhydrous citric acid and ethylenediamine were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Chemical reagent. All other reagents are of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. The standard Cr(VI) stock solution (3.0 mM) was prepared from K2Cr2O7. The other metal ion stock solutions were prepared from their nitrate or chloride salts.

The N-CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method. Typically, 2.0 g of anhydrous citric acid and 1.0 mL of ethylenediamine were dissolved in 40.0 mL of distilled water. Then, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave of 100 mL capacity. After hydrothermal treatment for 2 h at 150 °C, the resultant solution was subjected to dialysis (cutoff M n: 3.0 kDa) for 48 h at room temperature. Finally, the N-CDs were freeze-dried under vacuum to obtain a brown solid.

High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) was performed on JEM-2100F (JEOL, Japan). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained on a commercial AFM system (WET-SPM, Shimadzu Co., Japan). XRD patterns were performed on a Bruker D8 Focus diffractometer. The fluorescence spectra were obtained on an FLS920P Edinburgh Analytical Instrument (Edinburgh Instrument, UK). The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a Perkin–Elmer Lambder 25 spectrophotometer. FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 5700 FT-IR spectrophotometer using the KBr pellet technique. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer. The zeta potential was obtained on Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern instrument, UK) at 25 °C.

The quantum yield (QY) of N-CDs was measured using Rhodamine B (in ultrapure water, QY = 28.6%) as the standard. The QY of the N-CDs can be calculated by the following equation:
Φ =
Φ R
×
I
I R
×
A
A R
×
η 2
η R 2
where Φ is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated fluorescence emission intensity, A is the optical density, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript R refers to the standard with known QY.

The detection of Cr(VI) ion was performed at room temperature in ultrapure water solution. 3.0 mL of N-CDs solution (3.0 μg/mL) was added into a quartz cuvette, and then various concentrations of Cr(VI) ions were added stepwise to the N-CDs solution. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after the mixed solution reaction for 30 s. The selectivity for Cr(VI) ions was performed by adding other metal ions instead of Cr (VI) in a similar way.

To evaluate the N-CDs sensor for detection of Cr(VI) ion in environment waters, the real water samples obtained from Sha river (Zhoukou, Henan, China) were examined. The water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane prior to detection. The water samples were spiked with different volume of standard Cr(VI) ion solutions and then analyzed with the above described method.

In this study, the N-CDs were simply prepared by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and ethylenediamine for 2 h at 150 °C (Scheme 1 ). The citric acid and ethylenediamine can provide carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. The TEM image of N-CDs showed that the obtained N-CDs are spherical dots and exhibited a good dispersibility in water (Fig. 1A and B). The nanoparticle size distribution histogram (inset of Fig. 1A), which was determined by randomly counting one hundred twenty particles, exhibited that the average size of the N-CDs was about 6 nm. The HRTEM image exhibited the lattice spacing was ca. 0.25 nm (inset of Fig. 1B), which correspond to the (100) facet of sp2 graphitic carbon [1]. Form the XRD pattern in Fig. 2A, a broad peak around 22.8° can be observed, which correspond to the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials [33]. This observation can be ascribed to diffractions of disordered carbon atoms [19].

The surface groups of N-CDs were further investigated by FT-IR spectrometer and the results are displayed in Fig. 2B. As displayed in Fig. 2B, the broad absorption bands in the region of 3081–3408 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, and the band at 1400 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric stretching vibration of the carboxylate anions [39]. The peaks at 1717 and 1650 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO and CN groups, respectively [40]. The band at 1568 cm−1 was ascribed to the bending vibration of NH. The surface chemistry of N-CDs was also characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 3 ). As displayed in Fig. 3A, the typical intense peaks at 285.3, 399.2 and 531.8 eV can be observed for the N-CDs, which were ascribed to the binding energies of C1s, N1 s and O1s, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s are shown in Fig. 3B. Four peaks at around 284.6, 285.9, 287.2 and 288.4 eV can be observed from the Fig. 3B, which were ascribed to the CC/CC, CN, CO and CN/CO groups, respectively [1]. For the high-resolution spectrum of N1 s (Fig. 3C), the peaks at 399.8 and 401.0 eV were assigned to the absorption of CNC and NH groups, respectively [10,19]. This indicates that nitrogen element is doped into the carbon dots. The O1s spectrum exhibited two peaks at 531.5 and 532.9 eV (Fig. 3D), which can be assigned to the CO and COC/COH groups, respectively. The XPS data further confirm the presence of functional groups (e.g., oxygenate and nitrous) on the surface of N-CDs, which are in good agreement with the FT-IR results. The calculated zeta potential of the N-CDs was about −35.4 mV (Fig. S1), indicating that the surface of N-CDs was negatively charged due to the ionization of carboxyl or hydroxyl groups.

Fig. 4A exhibits the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of N-CDs. As can be seen from the UV–vis spectrum in Fig. 4A, two strong absorption bands at around 240 and 350 nm can be observed. The band at 240 nm due to the π-π* transitions of the carbonyl group (CC/CO) of N-CDs. The another band at 350 nm was assigned to the n-π* transitions of the amine moiety [30]. As depicted in Fig. 4A, N-CDs exhibited two excitation peaks at 257 and 350 nm and strong blue emission centered at 465 nm when excited at 350 nm (Fig. S2). Therefore, the excitation wavelength was selected at 350 nm in the subsequent fluorescence study. The presence of two excitation wavelength indicates that there are at least two types of excitation energy trapped on the N-CDs. It was found from Fig. S2 that the emission wavelength kept constant (465 nm) except for a significant decrease of fluorescence intensity when excited at 257 nm. A very bright blue fluorescence (inset of Fig. 4A) can be observed under a UV lamp (λ ex = 365 nm). Moreover, a bright blue light also can be observed even after being stored at room temperature for 3 months (Fig. S3), indicating the good photostability of the N-CDs. The QY of the N-CDs was measured at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, ant the QY was calculated to be about 58.6% with Rhodamine B as a standard, indicating the good fluorescence of the N-CDs. Such high QY ensure the good fluorescence emission even at a low concentration [41,42].

Fig. 4B shows the fluorescent emission spectra of the N-CDs under different excitation wavelengths. Unlike the previously reported N-CDs by Zhu et al. [19], the maximum emission wavelength (465 nm) remained constant when the excitation wavelength changes from 280 to 420 nm (Fig. 4B). The excitation-independent property of CDs makes them have potential applications in bioimaging and cell labeling because it can avoid the autofluorescence [39].

The feasibility of utilizing the N-CDs as chemsensor for detection of Cr (VI) ions was further investigated. As displayed in Fig. 5A, with increase of the Cr (VI) concentration, the fluorescence emission intensity of the N-CDs decreased gradually, revealing that the fluorescence of N-CDs is effectively quenched by Cr(VI) ions. In addition, the fluorescent intensity of N-CDs decreased significantly and kept constant within a reaction time of 30 s, indicating that Cr (VI) can fast quench the fluorescence of the N-CDs. Correspondingly, as displayed Fig. 5A (inset), the blue fluorescence emission almost disappeared under UV light of 365 nm after addition of Cr(VI) (60 μM) to the N-CDs. Fig. 5B displays the normalized PL intensity versus the concentration of Cr (VI) ions. A good linearity (R2 = 0.993) can be observed in the range of 0–10 μM (inset of Fig. 5B). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.26 μM based on three times the standard deviation rule, which was much lower than the maximum concentration (0.96 μM) of Cr(VI) in drinking water permitted by the WHO [4].

The fluorescence quenching mechanism was further investigated by UV–vis spectrophotometer and the results are displayed in Fig. 7 . From the UV–vis spectrum of Cr (VI) in Fig. 7A, it was found that the Cr(VI) exhibited two strong absorption bands at 257 nm and 350 nm, which precisely overlapped with the excitation wavelength at 257 and 350 nm of the N-CDs. Thus, the excitation wavelength for N-CDs can be absorbed by Cr(VI), which resulted in the fluorescence of N-CDs being effectively quenched by the absorber Cr(VI). This phenomenon is the so-called the inner filter effect (IFE). IFE effect can be occurred when the absorption band of the absorber and the excitation or emission band of the fluorophore are overlapped [1,35], which can remarkably enhance the sensitivity and selectivity compared to the other fluorescence quenching mechanism [18,35]. Thus, we conclude that the quenching mechanism is probably resulted from the inner filter effect.

Another quenching mechanism are possibly attributed to the electron transfer due to the strong interactions between Cr (VI) ions and the functional groups of the N-CDs. Unlike previous studies that there was no interactions between Cr(VI) ions and CDs [1], we found that the rich functional groups (COOH, OH and NH2 groups) of N-CDs can interact with Cr(VI) ions in this study. The UV–vis spectrometer was employed to confirm the presence of interactions between them and the results were shown in Fig. 7B. As displayed in Fig.7B, for the mixture of Cr(VI) and N-CDs (red line in Fig. 7B), the absorption peak of Cr(VI) at 257 nm were split into two peaks (245 and 272 nm) and the band at 350 nm was shifted to 360 nm. Correspondingly, the absorption peaks of N-CDs (the black dotted line in Fig. 7B) at 240 and 350 nm were shifted to 245 and 360 nm, respectively. These observations indicate that there exist strong interactions between Cr(VI) ions and the functional groups (e.g., COOH, OH and NH2 groups) of N-CDs. Thus, the fluorescence quenching was occurred owing to the nonradiative electron-transfer from the excited states of N-CDs to the unoccupied orbit of Cr (VI) [45]. From the above discussion, we can conclude that the fluorescence quenching mechanism possible results from the IFE effect and the electron transfer from the N-CDs to Cr(VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) in Environment Water
ce:section.ce:para: We then investigate the practical application of the N-CDs sensor for detection of Cr(VI) in the Sha river in our city. The various concentrations of Cr(VI) were added into the resultant water and analyzed with the proposed method. Fig. 8A exhibits the fluorescence response of the N-CDs in river water after adding of various concentrations of standard Cr(VI) solution. As shown in Fig. 8A, the fluorescence intensity (λ ex = 350 nm) gradually decreased with the increase of the concentration of Cr(VI) in river water from 0 to 40 μM. Moreover, as displayed in Fig. 8B, a good liner relationship (F0–F/F0 = 0.034C + 0.015, R2 = 0.99) was obtained in the range of 0–12 μM by plotting the F0–F/F0 versus the concentration of Cr(VI) ions. These results indicate that the obtained N-CDs can be employed a promising chemsensor for sensitive detection of Cr(VI) in environment water.

In conclusion, we developed a facile, mild hydrothermal method to prepare the highly fluorescent N-CDs using citric acid and ethylenediamine as co-precursor for detection of toxic Cr(VI) ions in aqueous solution. The obtained N-CDs exhibited high quantum yield (up to 58.6%), good photostability and excitation-independent emission behavior. Moreover, the N-CDs can be used as a fluorescent probe for fast and effective detection of Cr(VI) with a detection limit of 0.26 μM. Moreover, the experimental results reveal that the fluorescence quenching mechanism arise from the electron transfer from the N-CDs to Cr(VI) ions and the inner filter effect (IFE). We believe that the N-CDs can be served as a facile fluorescent sensing platform for detection of Cr(VI) ions and have promising prospect in environment applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21601213, 51572303), Innovation Scientists and Technicians Troop Construction Projects of Henan Province (2013259), the Key Program of Committee of Education of Henan Province (17A150055).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Additional Zeta potential, fluorescence emission spectra and the photograph of N-CDs are presented in the Supplementary Materials file. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.07.082.
